people.  Above and beyond direct material considera-
tions is the incalculable effect—a partly material effect,
too—that must result from the psychological effect of

- and—if we may put it that way—hopes of failure before

them, and make o material valuation of them; then

Home Rule; and that ‘difference, so far as it 1s con-
cerned with material things, would have to be expressed
in hundreds of millions sterling. What is the difference
between a man of pluck before a good potential busi-
ness proposition and a man of no pluck or half pluck?
It may be safely concluded that the latter will not be
a success: the degree of success of the former is the
only matter for speculation. An Irish Parliament and
power over our destinies to a considerable extent—in
these we have the forces that, on the material side,
must add untold millions to the potential possibilities
of the Trish nation ; and when we consider this aspect of
the case we regard quibbling over small details as we
regard the odd shillings and pence in a deal that in-
volved hundreds of pounds sterling.

Modern Ireland has suffered lieavily from the belief -

that ““ nothing Irish succeeds,”” and certainly in a
country whose population was leaving it yearly in tens

. of thousands, whose population was to a great extent

reared, as a matter of course, for emigration, 1t was
not to be wondered at that the view that nothing Irish
succeads was so strongly held in the country. It was
this national throwing up of hands that explains the
decay and stagnation of much in modern Ireland,
though, of course, the throwing up of hands in its turn
is the symptom of very many deeper causes. The Irish
Ireland movement, the Language movement, the In-
dustrial movement were attempts, brave and partially
successful attempts, to calm the panic and re-organise
the retreating forces. Some worthy men who
had grown old in the fight for Home Rule.
saw so clearly that without Home Rule no-
thing permanent could happen, that they brushed
aside, or were, perhaps, suspicious of the new
efforts to rally the nation; those of the newer move-
ments, or at least such of them as had insight, saw
that all was ultimately hopeless without Home Rule,
but they also saw clearly that a hope and belief in the
coming of Home Rule was irrational without a mean-
while attempt to strengthen and rally the country
whenever a chance offered. For a time there was & mis-
understanding—we suppose it was inevitable; but that
righted itself in time. John Dillon was, perhaps, the
outstanding figure in the old school, and—we make no
silly and hypocritical apology for saying it—we repre-
sented the new school, the school that saw that all was

lost if we did not ultimately get Home Rule, but that -

Home Rule was lost if we did not at once'set to stop
the leakages and strengthen the country whenever there
was a chance of doing so. We attacked raimeis, tin
pikery, and virulent anti-Catholic bigotry, “and
we started the TIndustrial Revival, and faced
boldly the attitude which Ireland should adopt.
towards the Crown. Ireland has made great progress
during the last ten years in industry, nationality and
in common sense, and humourously enough the silly
Unionists are attempting to use the material progress
ag an argument against Home Rule. We want Home
Rule in order to keep the country up to progress; and
we look to the psychological effect of Home Rule as
perhaps the greatest separate force amongst the many
that it involves for the strengthening and prospering
of Ireland, and that effect could not be expressed in
terms of money without going into hundreds of millions.

~ FUZZY SOURFACE.
o | (By Bloodbard’ Crippling.)
T’ve seen a lot of fighters here and there,

. And some of them were white and some were black;

The Fuzzy Wuzzies broke a British square
And proved themselves the pick of all the pack;
But not from Mandalay to Suakim
You'd find a big, black, bounding beggar tall
To matich an Irish Sourface, fierce and grim.
Oh, he’s the most terrific man of all.
Here’s to you, Fuzzy Sourface, who may soon with
N7 girded loin,
Display your warlike visage o’er the ditches of the

Boyne.
Oh, well may Roman legions quake, and rebels feel

~ despair
When Fuzzy Sourface makes a charge upon a Papist

square.

r

The loyal Rathmines Johnny is a tyke,
He’d prove himself a terror in the field;
The Thirty Shilling Dirty with a pike
Behind a muddy ditch would never yield.
The bounding cad of Trinity also
Is all hot sand and ginger against Rome;

- But 'tis in Portadown and Sandy Row

The real Fuzzy Sourface is at home.

Here’s to you, Fuzzy Sourface, cte.

To you, oh, Fuzzy Sourface, now at last

The mighty British Empire looks with hope
To save it from that combinationr vast

That wants to hold this island for the Pope.
So whether you do wear a wig and gown,

A shoddy coat, or garment all the go,
Be ready with your grimest look and frown

To England guard from England’s oldest foe.

Here’s to you, Fuzzy Sourface, etc.

Oh, never will the empire go to rack
While Fuzzy Sourface watches at his post,

With Bonar Law to pat him on the back

- And of his loyal truculence to boast.

He’s a daisy, he’s a demon in a fight, |
"He’s an India-rubber tiger on two legs,

And if he had a big stick, oh, he might
Break up a whole big basketful of eggs.

Here’s to you, Fuzzy Sourface, efc.

THE LEADER.

The fairies of Plunkett House are still engaged 1n

247

the endeavour to catch the State lepracaun with the
crock of gold. The public who follow Irish affairs in
any considerable detail must be tired of this absurd
effort to grab State funds in the name of self-help, the
while the would-be grabbers have mixed themselves up
with most objectionable anti-Irish forces and have
roused, and naturally roused, the Irish people against
them. If the Plunkett House and its hairy fairies con-

ducted the ordinary business of the I.A.O.S. with as

signal a want of tact and signal display of impertinence
as they displayed in: their latest attempt to grab public
money they must have made a sad mess of a great
opportunity. |

IFor our part the question as to whether an organisa-
tion society got £6,000 a year of State money—having
already consumed about £300,000 of public funds—or
did not get it, is a detail that would not interest us very
much, and we believe that if the Plunkett gang had
not by gross impertinence and want of ability and tact
gol up Lhe back of the country against them, the coun-
try would be as little concerned in the matter as we
would be. DBut there is a decided incompatibility of

temper, 1f we may put it that way, between the hairy .

fairies of Plunkett House and the Irish naftion. Fur-
thermore the alliance between the hairy fairies and the
““ Irish Times,’”’ and that class of gang in England as
well as Ireland, has created a new situation in which the
technical merits of the question become only counters
in the controversy: the reality now is that Ireland will
not be brow-beaten by the hungry and impudent gang
who are using poor Plunkett and whose fulsome flattery
of that well meaning weakling has been his undoing.
Plunkett and William O’Brien are two weakly-strong
men rendered impossible by the respective crowds of
alnti-Irish persons and venal flatterers who are using
them.

As for the capacity of the hairy fairies of Plunkett
House to injure the legitimate traders of Ireland even
with a State subsidy of ten times the amount that they
have been trying to grab, we have simply supreme con-
tempt. If Plunkett House had a reasonable amount of
ability and knowledge of the country the public would
treat them without hostility and would not be suffi-
ciently interested to agitate for or against them, and if
there was any business and go in them they might have
escaped serious opposition. -

The traders’ deputation that met at the College of
dclence was composed of rather large ‘‘ gombeen’’ men.
The traders of Ireland are-to the hairy fairies what the
A.O.H. is to the diseased imagination of poor screeching
William. William is hysterical against what he calls
the ** Mollies,”’ and the hairy fairies of Plunkett House
have worked themselves into a somewhat similar atti-
tude towards traders and are in a perpetual state of
shouting “‘gombeen.”” Poor William: unhappy hairy
fairies! The “‘gombeen’’ men of the deputation included
the Mayor of Limerick; My, McCowen, of Tralee; M.
R. Verner, of T. & J. McErvel, Ltd., Belfast; My. J.
Donohoe, of Enniscorthy ; Mr. W. Halliday, V.P. Lime-

rick Chamber of Commerce; Mr. J. Hamilton Hunter, .

Dublin; Mr. W. J. Smith, V.P. Waterford Chamber of
Commerce.

Of course the ‘ Irish Times '’ backed up the hairy
fairies: probably it was one of the hairy fairies who
wrote the article. There was a weak jibe at the National-
ist Party. ¢ Lastly, we have the Irish Nationalist
Party, which opposes Sir Horace Plunkett’s movement

- why the Parliament should necessarily clect to plant
itself in the congested centre of the city without room

the House should be in the heart of the city at all.
+  For instance, a fine site, with a magnificent view,
might be obtained in the Pheenix Park; or a site
with terrace overlooking the south side of Dublin Bay
-would be likewise admirable.  The Imperial Parlia-
ment is not in the city of London; but in Westminster.
Any suitable site within a radius of five or six miles from
the centre of Dublin would serve. By the way, it is
rumowred that Mr. W. M. Murphy, ‘* The Rebel,"”” 1s
holding Carlisle Buildings on the chance that, if the
Bank of Ineland leaves College Green, he will be able to
ofter the Bank his newly-acquired premises at a hand-
some profit. We do not know whether there is any
truth in that or not. If Ireland wants the College
Green house it is rather absurd to suggest that any
Bank, depending upon Irish custom, would refuse to
agive it , for there are so many other Banks, 1
cluding the Banks in the North, that would
be glad to take up any customers that might
leave a Bank that put itself needlessly and
wantonly  against the wishes of the nation.
We were only in the ** Old House '’ once, and our re-
collection of it is not very vivid; it was all right for the
eighteenth century, but whether it is a desivable site
—  and building for a Parliament in the twentieth century
is another question. We are inclined to think that
during such a summer as last summer it would be a
most undesirable and stuffy building. A fine airy
place, backing on Dublin Bay, appeals more kindly to
the rational man. Perhaps, the Clontarf and Dolly-
mount Improvement Committee will be putting in a
claim for the North Bull as a site! It would be the
making of Clontarf, and the vicinity of Clontarf would
be historically appropriate ; but we are inclined to thinlk
. that the south side of the Bay would have preponderat-
| ine advantages. What hag the Bank of Ireland to re-
commend it, but some half-glorious and some most -
oJorious historical associations. We have great respeet
for historical associations, but we would be sorry to be
<o weak-minded as to allow ourselves to be dominated
by them.

Cackling Kettle has deserted the Suffers; he is now
an emancipated male—at least for the time being.
Things became rather hot, and after the setting out of
the case with regard to the Convention in our last 1ssue
the hob became unbearable—but we will get mixed In
our metaphors if we go on like this. Skeffy, the Car- £
vion Sparrow, stands on the burning hob when all but
he had fled. If the Suffers do not make an Aunt Sally
of their kitchen utensil they will curtail our amusement
and drop the curtain when the comedy is becoming most
interesting. The suffering sandwich women should
serenade Kettle now. Where is Cruso all this time ?
Has he deserted, too? Et {u Cruso? Where are the
long-haired poets ? If the Suffers want to kick up ruc-
tions we fear they must not rely on the help of Kettle
and Oldham and Cruso—and ¢ Kelly and Burke and
Shea ’’ are only laughing at them.

~  The Catholic Young Men’s Society of Kilkenny, a
town that was recently described as ‘‘ the moribund

~ hamlet of marble and mud,” was until recently trying
to die. Were it not for a few of its senior members
the clubs—with bars attached—would long ago have
-absorbed many of the younger members. Some
-months ago an effort was made to revive the society,
and, with a view to raising funds, it was decided to
hold a “ Grand Concert and Dramatic Entertainment ™
in the local theatre. The entertainment was boomed
with great vigour, and in' due time it came off. A
correspondent tells us that: ‘“ The matter was alto-
gether un-Irish, and though the caste of the first piece
was small, tolerance proving demanded that two of the
characters should be non-Catholic. A caste so consti-
- tuted is a poor tribute to the social capabilities of the
society, and the whole performance reflects seriously
on the National and Catholic spirit of the community.”’
We fear Kilkenny is a town of little spirit. =~ The two
plays were “ As Mad as a Hatter”’ and " Crazed.”
Needless to say there was no song in Irish. Why not
some local people write and stage a play to be called
“ Daft: or, Under the Shadow of Ormonde Castle.”’

A great big leak has been sprung in the All-for-I. of
Cork, and men, including locally eminent men, are
leaking out of it. We may be cxpecting letters in the
papers now suggesting, with mawkish sentimentality,
that ‘‘it is time to close up our ranks’’ and have a
reconciliation between Screeching William and the
Party. The time for that has gone by. Screeching
William has done his spiteful and bitler worst, and he
has been well beaten, and let him take his beating now.
In the last few critical years the unhappy man, in his
impotent rage, has been screeching to the delight of the
Carrion Crows and doing his little worst to malke things
_as unpleasant as possible for the Nationalists. The un-
fortunate and feather-headed men who sided with the
Mallow Mullah are now left in a pitiable plight; and
not having the screeching and self-deluding capacities
of the Mullah, they must be feeling extremely ridi-
culous. William has led them on a pretty march; no
doubt the poor fellows thought that William would lead
them to glory, and now there they are with nothing to
do but leak out of ‘° Rotten Cork ” into ordinary every-
day honest Cork as quickly and as quietly as they can.
““* Rotten Cork ’ will soon be non-existent, and the
rank and file of it will have merged into the ranks of
Nationalist Cork. The All-for-I. is as good as dead.

When the Protessor finds it convenient to alter his
course he emits a cloud of words. The declaration of
war on the Party, the threat that militant tactics would
result if the Convention did not toe the line, were all
before the public when the Professor volunteered to
propose his Feminist Resolution, and now he seems to
have backed down; he tried to use some article in an
Fnglish paper, called * Votes for Wqmen,” as a new
excuse for hig twist, but the declaration of war on the
Trish Party and the intimidatory threat to the Con-
vontion were before him when he volunteered to pro-
pose his resolution. The Professor gave out‘ a s_LaLe,;
ment, as the Americans say, to a man from °‘ Pink
on Monday, and with cool assurance, at which 1t 1s
wiser to smile than to adopt another alternative, he

took it upon himself to advise the delegates of the
Irish nation how to behave towards wonien! Is he

then Professor of National Manners! The delegates
will be grateful to be assured by the Professor that they
““ are not Cockney women-baiters,”’” whatever the latter
may be. The Professor would be better employed if
he told the members of the Association, who probably
look upon him now as a man who ratted, that it is not in
keeping with ‘‘ the old, traditional courtesy of Ire-
land,”’ for women to go about interrupting national
meetings, threatening * militancy *’ and turning them-
selves into sandwich paraders and giving joy to the
enemies of Ireland.

As Tuesday is the day we go to press we could only
find time to pay a brief visit to the great Convention.
We walked up Dawson Street before eleven o’clock ex-
pecting to see Skeffy heading some surging thousands
of Shrieking Suffers. But we found no trace of Skefiy,
and the two batches of Suffers present scarcely num-
bered thirty. KEach batch—-we hope strangers did not
take them as representative of the women of Ireland—
was headed off by a few stalwart policemen. It was a
ridiculous anti-climax to all the threats and letters that
have been bandied about. We saw no trace of Kettle
or Cruso: nor did we see Oldham.

Before we entered the gate to the Mansion House
we had a good look at the two small bands of Suffers.

Were they about to storm the Convention! In due
course there was a little scene: the police evi-

dently had occasion to push-back the first little gang of
Suffers: then there was just a slight commotion and a
few shrieks and all was quiet again. We left the
crowds in the street and the Suffers to look after them-
selves and went into the Convention.

of our visit being only a brief one had its advantages,
for we did not envy those who had penctrated so far
into the Round Room that they could not get out. The
heat was stifling, and this even over half an hour before
the commencement of the proceedings. Delegates were

It must be a new development in military strategy when
it helps a commander to have a party harassing his
flank, and another cutting his communications in the
rear.

However, ‘‘ out of evil cometh good '"; this All-for-
Ireland business has not been an unmixed curse. At
the precise moment the trouble arose, the country was
inglined to slumber, and the All-for-Ireland poison In
the body politic roused it from its lethargy. But let
not Mr. O’Brien and his followers claim credit for that.

Now that the wisdom of Mr. Redmond’s foresight and
generalship has been abundantly proved, there is a dis-
position among the better-informed and more patriotic
of Mr. O’Brien’s following to throw in their lot with
the vast majority of their fellow-countrymen.  This
feeling is bound to grow. Ireland needs all her sons,
and they shall be heartily welcome. In this connec-

is now what it has ever becn: Win the fight or pass the
banner on. The curly heads that Mitchel dreamt of
have done their part, and there are curly heads in the
cradles of Ireland to-day—Davitts’ and Parnells’ and

N the_p()sivtion of the layman in the governing of the
~ old Catholic University. Before going further, how-
ever, I should like to say that the views of Newman
and Cullen re the function of this University were so
dissimilar that it would have been a miracle if either
was satisfied with the work of the other. To those
reading between the lines of Newman’s life and writ-
mgs, 1t s evident that what he dreamt of was
a Catholic University in Treland, and not an
Irish Catholic University. He wanted a Univer-
sity which would be to Catholics—especially the
new converts in England—a substitute and, as
1t were, a counterpart of the Oxford University
they had lost, and which themselves and their children
i siched for. That it happened to be in Ireland was the
result of an accident fatal to Newman's ideas and to
the University. Had he succeeded in his aim we
- * should have had foisted on us as a final denationalizing
‘ influence, a University alien in tone, in spirit, and in
culture, and unsuited to the economical and educational
wants of Ireland. Cullen never accepted Newman's
ideals.

To those at all acquainted with the government of
this institution for the first twenty years it is evident
that what Cullen desired and aimed at, and, as Dele-
cate Apostolie, finally succeeded in getting his brother
bishops to support him in, was the foundation, not of a
Catholic University, but-of a glorified Ecclesiastical
Seminary.

Let me, in support of this view, give a few authenti-
cated facts; they will at the same time show your con-
tributor, ‘“ Y.,”’ that the layman got but a poor show

" in the government of the institution in Stephen’s Green
as conceived and worked by Cullen.

In the Calendar of the Catholic University for 1866
we find the following: ‘‘ In order to carry out the wishes
and intentions of his Holiness Pope Pius IX., a com-
mittee was appointed in 1859 consisting of the four
Archbishops, four suffragan Bishops, eight other eccle-
siastics, and eight (!) laymen.”” ‘Imagine the situa-
tion: two-thirds of the Senate were ecclesiastics, and
but one-third, and that the weakest, laymen.  Little
wonder that, under such circumstances, Newman, with
his experience of Oxford and his knowledge of the work-
ing of Universities in other parts of the world, should

! think Cullen ‘‘ narrow.”’” Is ‘“Y.” serious when he
wishes us to believe that the layman had any chance of
asserting himself in the old Catholic University?
Second fact: While the names of all the episcopal
members of the Senate of this University are found in
the Calendar, we search in vain for the names of the
other ecclesiastics and of the laymen. In Cullen’s
mind Jaymen existed solely to supply funds and fresh-
men to this institution, managed by himself and the
other Bishops. = One rejoiced that Newman failed in
founding an English University in Ireland. One equally
rejoices that Cullen’s glorified Ecclesiastical Seminary

AN APPARITION.

One evening about five years ago I went out as usual
for a stroll after dinner. I had gone a couple of miles
from home, and was turning homewards again, when
I heard a very peculiar noise behind me. Now this same
road on which I was then walking is famous for the
number of ghosts who are said to patrol it, and who, if
the local tradition is to be believed, are not precise about
waiting for the midnight hour to take their constitutionals.
It was now about seven o’clock, dark and foggy ; the
noise—which was neither a creak, nor a groan, nor a
rattle, nor a rumble, but seemed to be a combination of
all—continued to draw nearer and increased steadily in
volume. Under these circumstances I am not ashamed
to confess that fear took possession of me, and with it
an idea that Satan himself, or at lecast one of his right-
hand men, was  approaching. The idea was further
strengthened when the fearful creature drew near, for

it seemed to be mounted upon some kind of an infernal

machine, which it propelled as you would a bicycle.

properties of both. Altogether it was a terrible vehicle,
and appeared to be too big and heavy to be propelled
by any ordinary human being. The creature who was

We see by the evening papers that the great Con-
vention endorsed the Home Rule offer with unanimity
and enthusiasm. The main resolution was as follows :—

That we welcome the Government of Ireland
Bill as an honest and generous attempt to settle
the long and disastrous quarrel between the Bri-
tish and Irish nations; and this National Conven-
tion of the Irish people decides to accept the Bill
in the spirit in which it is offered: and we hereby
declare our solemn convietion that the passage of
this Bill into law will bind the people of Treland to
the people of Great Britain by a union infinitely
closer than that which now exists and by so doing
add immeasurably to the strength of the Iimpire.

It was a happy and pleasant arrangement that the reso-
lution was seconded by the Lord Mavor of Cork. “* So
far as Cork is concerned,’”’ said his lordship, *“ we all
know that Cork is coming right.” 1

IRISH PIPING.

I wish a full attendance of Irish Pipers at the
Oireachtas this year—individual pipers and bands of
pipers. Piping in Ireland has made marvellous strides
within the past few years, and is now firmly established.
This is the time when a pipers’ league can be formed,
a fully representative and authoritative body, regulating
all matters dealing with the piob mor. At the same
time competlitions for bands and single pipers can be
held. A special day can be allocated for this purpose,
with judges and advisers to assist in music, model of
pipe, costume and any other featurc tending to promote
this aspect of the Gaelic Revival. Suggestions and
advice, tending to advance the cause so many Gaels
have at heart, will be heartily welcome.
